description: Expanded PhD-level knowledge pack on the intersection of artificial intelligence, psychology, and cognitive science for Marion. This file emphasizes computational cognitive modeling, human-AI interaction, trust and reliance, behavioral prediction, affective computing, clinical and decision-support considerations, and research frameworks that connect AI systems to human cognition and behavior.

theoretical_frames.name: computational_cognitive_science
theoretical_frames.importance: Provides the strongest doctoral bridge between AI and psychology by treating cognition as something that can be formalized, simulated, and compared against human data.

theoretical_frames.name: human_centered_ai
theoretical_frames.importance: Keeps the human as the locus of agency, interpretation, accountability, and value alignment.

theoretical_frames.name: trust_and_reliance_calibration
theoretical_frames.importance: Explains when people under-trust, over-trust, or miscalibrate their use of AI and automation.

theoretical_frames.name: bounded_rationality_and_bias
theoretical_frames.importance: Helps Marion reason about human decision limits, cognitive shortcuts, and where AI can either mitigate or amplify bias.

theoretical_frames.name: social_cognition_of_ai
theoretical_frames.importance: Explains why people anthropomorphize AI, project agency onto systems, and respond to conversational systems as quasi-social actors.

primary_text_references.title: The Cambridge Handbook of Computational Psychology
The Oxford Handbook of Cognitive Science

reference_books_and_handbooks.title: The Cambridge Handbook of Computational Cognitive Sciences
The Oxford Handbook of Computational and Mathematical Psychology
The Oxford Handbook of Cognitive Psychology

foundational_papers_and_reviews.title: Integrating Empirical Evidence on Factors That Influence Trust in Automation
From Automation to Autonomy: The Importance of Trust Repair in Human-Machine Interaction
Understanding Human-Centred AI: A Review of Its Defining Characteristics

case_study_references.title: Trust Repair in Human-Machine Interaction
case_study_references.why_it_matters: Important for Marion because failure recovery, apology strategy, and confidence calibration are psychological as much as technical.

case_study_references.title: Human Decision-Making Susceptibility to AI-Driven Manipulation
case_study_references.why_it_matters: Useful for understanding persuasion risk, hidden incentive effects, and why AI systems need guardrails around influence.

case_study_references.title: APA Guidance on AI and Psychological Practice
case_study_references.why_it_matters: Provides a cautionary frame for clinical and wellness-adjacent deployment, especially around evidence, privacy, and public safety.

case_study_references.title: Psychologists' Reported AI Use and Concerns
case_study_references.why_it_matters: Helpful for understanding real-world adoption, concern patterns, and the psychology profession's risk/benefit framing around AI.

advanced_subtopics.why_it_matters: Architectures such as ACT-R, EPIC, CAPS-family, and CLARION matter because they give Marion a principled way to think about cognition instead of treating behavior as undifferentiated output.
This is central to whether users appropriately rely on AI, ignore it when they should not, or over-trust it when they should.
People do not use AI based only on objective performance; they use it based on how they understand what it is doing.
A PhD-level psychology pack cannot ignore the possibility that AI can shape beliefs, preferences, compliance, and affect.
Psychological AI claims are weak if constructs are ill-defined, labels are noisy, or measurements lack validity and reliability.
AI systems are frequently over-extended into mental-health-adjacent settings without the evidence base required for safe use.

Integrating Empirical Evidence on Factors That Influence Trust in Automation

Understanding Human-Centred AI: A Review of Its Defining Characteristics

notes_for_marion: Keep this pack conceptually rigorous. It should function as a bridge between AI systems thinking and psychological theory, not as a loose collection of pop-psych claims.
Treat emotion detection and behavioral prediction as measurement problems, not just capability claims.
Separate supportive conversational design from clinical psychology claims unless the evidence base is explicit and strong.
Cross-link this pack later into agents, ethics/law, education, and decision-support systems.

strengths: Valid JSON and coherent top-level object structure.
Clear bridge into human-centered AI, trust calibration, and conversational design.
Runtime path and registry-consumption metadata are now explicit.
issues: Bibliographic/source structures are present, but direct source URLs/DOIs/ISBNs are not attached in-file.
Direct source URLs/DOIs/ISBNs are still pending where not already present; no source identifiers were fabricated.
recommendedActions: Attach authoritative source URLs, DOI, ISBN, or publisher identifiers to reference entries.
Keep manifest and pack versions synchronized.
Use alias routing when layer labels differ from case-study domain labels.
Preserve bibliographic references without embedding copyrighted chapters.

qualityAudit.remainingExternalDependency: Router must honor domain_question + knowledgeDomain=ai before technical_debug for definition prompts.

answerShape: one short explanation of AI-system relevance
clarifying branch only when the term is genuinely cross-domain
answerPolicy.technicalDebugPrecedenceRule: AI/domain definition prompts outrank technical_debug unless the user explicitly asks for autopsy, code, script, endpoint, patch, or validation.
answerPolicy.humanOversightBoundary: For AI governance, law, psychology, security, and high-impact decision support, keep human oversight and source limits visible.

ambiguityPolicy.template: Give a neutral definition, name the plausible AI subdomain interpretations, then ask which angle the user wants.

crossDomainBranches.ai_cybersecurity: security of AI systems, prompt injection, model/data integrity, and tool-use control
crossDomainBranches.ai_psychology: human cognition, trust, human-AI interaction, mental models, and affective computing
crossDomainBranches.ai_marketing: personalization, segmentation, recommendation, lifecycle automation, and measurement
crossDomainBranches.ethics_law: regulation, liability, privacy, IP, due process, and governance
crossDomainBranches.agents_systems: agent architecture, tools, memory, planning, orchestration, and evaluation
crossDomainBranches.foundations: core AI concepts such as learning, search, optimization, uncertainty, and evaluation

blockPhrases: I need one more concrete detail to route this cleanly.

Schedule depends on where you are.

runtimeDefinitionIndex.definition: Cognitive intelligence generally means the ability to understand information, reason through problems, learn from patterns, adapt to context, and make useful decisions.
Human-AI interaction studies how people understand, trust, use, resist, and collaborate with AI systems.
Trust calibration means aligning a user’s trust in an AI system with the system’s actual reliability, limits, and uncertainty.
Affective computing is the study and design of systems that detect, model, or respond to human emotion while respecting measurement limits and context.
A mental model is the user’s internal understanding of how a system works, what it can do, and when it can fail.
Cognitive load is the mental effort required to process information, make decisions, or complete a task.
Human-centered AI designs systems around human agency, context, accountability, usability, and real-world impact rather than capability alone.

crossDomainAmbiguousTerms.definition: Risk generally means exposure to possible harm, loss, uncertainty, or failure.
crossDomainAmbiguousTerms.clarifier: Do you mean security risk, legal/governance risk, business/marketing risk, or model-performance risk?

crossDomainAmbiguousTerms.definition: Validation means checking whether a model, system, claim, or workflow actually performs as intended under relevant conditions.
crossDomainAmbiguousTerms.clarifier: Do you mean model validation, security validation, legal/governance validation, or psychological measurement validation?

crossDomainAmbiguousTerms.definition: Alignment means making AI behavior match intended goals, user needs, policy boundaries, and human values.
crossDomainAmbiguousTerms.clarifier: Do you mean technical alignment, agent behavior alignment, governance alignment, or human trust/mental-model alignment?

crossDomainAmbiguousTerms.definition: Agency means the capacity to act toward goals; in AI, it usually refers to how much a system can plan, choose tools, and execute actions.
crossDomainAmbiguousTerms.clarifier: Do you mean agent autonomy, legal/ethical responsibility, or human perception of agency?
